nodes.name: 污染
nodes.summary: 农药、工业污染与水体污染影响食物链安全。

nodes.name: 鹤形目
nodes.summary: 鹤形目（Gruiformes）是图谱中的目级分类节点，当前关联126种鸟类，如丹顶鹤、灰冠鹤、Grey-winged trumpeter等126种鸟类。

nodes.name: 菲律宾
nodes.summary: 菲律宾是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联131种鸟类，包括细斑树鸭、青头潜鸭、菲律宾冢雉等131种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 猎捕压力
nodes.summary: 直接猎捕或误捕造成额外死亡。

nodes.name: 澳大利亚
nodes.summary: 澳大利亚是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联221种鸟类，包括南方鶴鴕、鵲鵝、细斑树鸭等221种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 食物资源下降
nodes.summary: 底栖生物和小型鱼类下降带来的觅食压力。

nodes.name: Masauji Hachisuka
nodes.summary: Masauji Hachisuka是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联2种鸟类，包括赤颈鹤、绿脸鹦雀，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 比哈尔邦
nodes.summary: 比哈尔邦是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联2种鸟类，包括白鹤、赤颈鹤，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 东南亚
nodes.summary: 东南亚是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联160种鸟类，包括棉鳧、琵嘴鸭、勺嘴鹬等160种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 湿地
nodes.summary: 浅水、沼泽与植被斑块组成的高生产力生态系统。

nodes.name: VU
nodes.summary: VU表示 IUCN 易危等级，当前关联694种鸟类，如丹顶鹤、索马里鸵鸟、大斑几维鸟等694种鸟类。

nodes.name: 人类活动扰动
nodes.summary: 旅游、施工和高频活动影响繁殖或觅食。

nodes.name: 栖息地破坏
nodes.summary: 开发、采伐和围垦等活动导致适宜生境减少。

nodes.name: 赤颈鹤
nodes.summary: 赤颈鹤（Sarus crane，学名 Antigone antigone），分类上属于鹤形目，并归入鹤科，在图谱中标记为易危（VU）物种，已关联8个分布地点节点，常见栖息地包括湿地，主要威胁包括栖息地破坏、食物资源下降、污染等5项。

nodes.name: 北方邦
nodes.summary: 北方邦是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联3种鸟类，包括赤颈鹤、斑椋鸟、岸八哥，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 鹤科
nodes.summary: 鹤科（Gruidae）是图谱中的科级分类节点，当前关联11种鸟类，如丹顶鹤、灰冠鹤、黑冕鶴等11种鸟类。

nodes.name: 旁遮普地区
nodes.summary: 旁遮普地区是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联2种鸟类，包括赤颈鹤、长耳鸮，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: Maharana Pratap Sagar
nodes.summary: Maharana Pratap Sagar是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括赤颈鹤，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: The species no longer breeds in Punjab, though it winters regularly in the state.{{cite journal|author1=Bal, R

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: |year=1998|title= Mammals, birds and reptiles of Dibru-Saikhowa Sanctuary, Assam, India|journal= Oryx|volume= 32|issue= 3|pages= 192–200|doi=10.1017/S0030605300029951|doi-access= free}} and are no longer found in the state of Bihar

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: |year=2003 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/256476085 |title= A review of the three subspecies of Sarus Cranes Grus antigone |journal=Journal of Ecological Society |volume=16 |pages=5–15|doi=10.54081/JES.015/01 |doi-access=free }} and the distinct, likely-extinct luzonica subspecies once found in the Philippines. The sarus used to extend to Thailand and further east into the Philippines, but may now be extinct in both these countries

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: 2|author=Vigne, GT|year=1842|publisher=Henry Colburn |place=London |url=https://archive.org/stream/travelsinkashmir02vign#page/18/mode/2up}} The sarus crane breeds in some high elevation regions such as near the Pong Dam in Himachal Pradesh, where populations may be growing in response to increasing rice cultivation along the reservoir. In rice-dominated districts of Uttar Pradesh, sarus crane abundance (estimated as occupancy) was highest in the western districts, intermediate in the central districts, and minimal in the eastern districts

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: 2|author=Vigne, GT|year=1842|publisher=Henry Colburn |place=London |url=https://archive.org/stream/travelsinkashmir02vign#page/18/mode/2up}} The sarus crane breeds in some high elevation regions such as near the Pong Dam in Himachal Pradesh, where populations may be growing in response to increasing rice cultivation along the reservoir. In rice-dominated districts of Uttar Pradesh, sarus crane abundance (estimated as occupancy) was highest in the western districts, intermediate in the central districts, and minimal in the eastern districts

links.label: 栖息于
links.evidence: |journal=Sunbird: Journal of the Queensland Ornithological Society| volume= 27|issue=3| year= 1997|pages= 72–78}} Flocks in the non-breeding season are commonly seen in the Atherton Tablelands in northeastern Queensland. border|left|thumb|Saras Pair In India, sarus cranes preferentially use wetlands for nesting, but also nest in uncultivated patches amid flooded rice paddies (called khet-taavadi in Gujarat), and in the rice paddies especially when wetlands are not available to breeding pairs. Breeding pairs are territorial and prefer to forage in natural wetlands, though wetland crops such as rice and wheat are also frequented. In south-western Uttar Pradesh, sarus cranes were found in wetlands of all sizes with larger numbers in larger wetlands.{{cite journal|author1=Sundar, K.S.G

links.label: 受威胁于
links.evidence: |title=In nature's garden |publisher=John Lane|place=London|year=1922 |url=https://archive.org/stream/innaturesgarden00donaiala#page/198/mode/2up|pages=199–208}} The role of rice paddies and associated irrigation structures may be particularly important for the birds' conservation, considering that natural wetlands are increasingly threatened by human activity. The conversion of wetlands to farmland, and farmland to more urban uses are major causes for habitat loss and long-term population decline. Compensating farmers for crop losses has been suggested as a helpful measure, but needs to be implemented judiciously so as not to corrupt and remove existing local traditions of tolerance. Farmers in Sarus crane wintering areas in Australia are beginning to use efficient methods to harvest crops, which may lead to lowered food availability

links.label: 受威胁于
links.evidence: |title=In nature's garden |publisher=John Lane|place=London|year=1922 |url=https://archive.org/stream/innaturesgarden00donaiala#page/198/mode/2up|pages=199–208}} The role of rice paddies and associated irrigation structures may be particularly important for the birds' conservation, considering that natural wetlands are increasingly threatened by human activity. The conversion of wetlands to farmland, and farmland to more urban uses are major causes for habitat loss and long-term population decline. Compensating farmers for crop losses has been suggested as a helpful measure, but needs to be implemented judiciously so as not to corrupt and remove existing local traditions of tolerance. Farmers in Sarus crane wintering areas in Australia are beginning to use efficient methods to harvest crops, which may lead to lowered food availability

links.label: 受威胁于
links.evidence: Nest success of protected nests was significantly higher than that of unprotected nests, and positive population-level impacts were apparent. However, the program also caused local jealousies leading to deliberate disturbance of nests, and did nothing to alleviate larger-scale and more permanent threats due to habitat losses leading to the conclusion that such payment-for-conservation programs are at best a short-term complement, and not a substitute, to more permanent interventions that include habitat preservation. The little-known Philippine population became extinct in the late-1960s. The sarus crane is classified as vulnerable on the IUCN Red List. Threats listed include habitat destruction and/or degradation, hunting and collecting, and environmental pollution, and possibly diseases or competing species
